title: Build Approaches Classification & Dashboard Tab
prompt: Implement the following plan: Build Approaches Classification & Dashboard Tab. Classify all upstream font repos by build system and source format, generate JSON data, and add a new dashboard tab to visualize results. User also requested: keep updating the website gradually, add an intro explaining each approach, and document plans on the website with timestamps.
summary: Create a Python script to classify ~1,664 upstream font repositories by build approach (gftools-builder, fontmake, custom scripts, etc.) and source format (.glyphs, .ufo, .designspace, etc.), then add a new dashboard tab with charts, filters, and descriptions.

steps.description: Create scripts/classify_build_approaches.py - Python script that scans all repos in the cache and classifies by build system (11 categories, priority-based) and source formats (non-exclusive detection).

Run the script to generate data/build_approaches.json with summary stats and per-repo entries. Ran in degraded mode due to filesystem open files limit - stat-only probing. Source format detection limited.
Add Build Approaches tab to index.html with summary cards, canvas charts, description cards for each approach, filter bar, and data table.
Add CSS styles to css/style.css for builds tab: summary grid, chart containers, category badges, source format tags, description cards, table styles.
Add JavaScript to js/main.js: loadBuildApproaches(), chart rendering, filtering, table rendering, and category formatting functions.
Document plans on the website - created Plans tab with timestamps and original prompts.
Verify and test: ran classification script in degraded mode, validated JSON output. Full re-scan with source format detection pending filesystem recovery.

build_categories.label: Source only (no build)
build_categories.detection: Has source files but no build system

build_categories.label: Pre-built only
build_categories.detection: Has .ttf/.otf but no sources or build system

title: Rust Reproducible Build System (fontc_crater fork)
prompt: Prototype the same reproducible build system currently implemented in Python, but written in Rust as a modification of fontc_crater. Keep the existing Python system unchanged. Later decide whether to follow the plan.
summary: Adapt fontc_crater — the Rust-based font compiler comparison tool in the googlefonts/fontc monorepo — into a reproducible build verification system for Google Fonts. Instead of comparing fontc vs fontmake output, the modified tool would compare rebuilt fonts against the binaries currently shipped in google/fonts, replicating what the Python build system does but in Rust with fontc_crater's existing parallelism, caching, and reporting infrastructure.

design.motivation: The current Python reproducible build system (gftools-builder + custom comparison scripts) works but is hard to run in CI and lacks a unified tool for orchestration, caching, and reporting. fontc_crater already has well-tested infrastructure for these: git cache management, result caching, HTML report generation, and CI integration (skip-if-unchanged). Repurposing it avoids reinventing that infrastructure. Both systems would face the same virtiofs FD accumulation bottleneck on the current VM — the Rust rewrite does not solve that.
design.current_fontc_crater_overview: fontc_crater takes a JSON list of upstream repos + commit hashes, clones them, compiles each source with both fontc and fontmake, then diffs the outputs via ttx_diff (a Python module). It produces per-target similarity ratios, failure reports, and an HTML dashboard. Key components: Target (repo+commit+config+source), CI pipeline (ci.rs), TTX diff runner (ttx_diff_runner.rs), source discovery (sources.rs), HTML reporting (ci/html.rs), result caching (ci/results_cache.rs).
design.current_python_build_system_overview: The Python system reads METADATA.pb for each family in google/fonts to find upstream repo URL, commit hash, and config.yaml path. It downloads the source at that commit, runs gftools-builder to compile fonts, then compares the built .ttf/.otf files against the binaries in google/fonts using SHA256 hashes and TTX table-by-table diffing. Results are categorized (byte-identical, compiler-version, name-table, timestamp-diff, build-failure, etc.) and reflow risk is assessed by checking advance width differences. Results feed into the gfonts_agents dashboard.
key_differences_to_bridge: fontc_crater compares fontc vs fontmake output; the new tool compares rebuilt fonts vs google/fonts shipped binaries
fontc_crater discovers targets from a hand-curated JSON list; the new tool must discover targets from METADATA.pb source blocks
fontc_crater uses ttx_diff (Python) for comparison; the new tool needs binary hash comparison + TTX table diffing + reflow risk analysis
fontc_crater tracks compiler parity; the new tool tracks reproducibility and categorizes root causes
fontc_crater only needs upstream repos; the new tool also needs a local clone of google/fonts for reference binaries

steps.description: Fork fontc_crater into a new crate (e.g., gfonts_repro_crater) within a local workspace. Keep fontc_crater's Cargo.toml dependencies (rayon, chrono, serde, clap, tempfile, maud) and add: protobuf or a simple METADATA.pb parser, sha2 (for SHA256 hashing), fonttools-rs or ttx bindings for table-level diffing.
steps.details: The fork should be a separate binary crate, not a modification of fontc_crater itself, so it can diverge freely. Copy the src/ directory and Cargo.toml, rename the package.

steps.description: Replace the Target struct. Current Target has: repo_dir, sha, config, is_virtual, source, build_type (Default|GfTools). New Target needs: family_slug (e.g. 'ofl/roboto'), repo_url, commit_hash, config_yaml_path, source_files (list), reference_binaries (paths to .ttf/.otf in google/fonts), license_dir. The new Target should be constructable from a METADATA.pb source block.
steps.details: The Target must carry enough info to: (a) check out the upstream repo at the right commit, (b) locate the config.yaml, (c) run gftools-builder or fontc, (d) find the reference binaries in google/fonts for comparison. Families without a source block (the 598 identified) are skipped.

steps.description: Replace source discovery (sources.rs). Instead of reading a hand-curated JSON file, walk all METADATA.pb files under google/fonts/{ofl,apache,ufl,cc-by-sa}/*/METADATA.pb. Parse each for 'source { repository_url, commit, config_yaml, files { source_file, dest_file } }'. Skip families with no source block. Build a Vec<Target> from the parsed data.
steps.details: METADATA.pb is a text-format protobuf. Parsing options: (a) use the protobuf crate with the FamilyProto .proto schema from google/fonts, (b) write a simple line-based parser since we only need a few fields, (c) shell out to Python's google.protobuf. Option (b) is simplest for a prototype. The parser needs to handle nested 'source { ... }' and 'files { ... }' blocks.

steps.description: Replace the TTX diff runner (ttx_diff_runner.rs) with a reproducibility comparison module. The new module: (a) runs gftools-builder (or fontc) on the checked-out source to produce built fonts, (b) locates reference binaries in google/fonts using the files { source_file, dest_file } mapping, (c) compares built vs reference via SHA256 first, (d) if hashes differ, runs ttx-based table comparison, (e) categorizes the result.
steps.details: For the prototype, the build step can shell out to gftools-builder (Python) — the same as the current system. The Rust advantage comes from orchestration, parallelism, and caching, not from reimplementing the compiler. Later, fontc could replace fontmake as the compiler. The comparison logic should produce a ReproResult enum: ByteIdentical, CompilerVersionDiff(tables), NameTableOnly, TimestampOnly, BuildFailure(error), ReflowRisk(details).

steps.description: Add reflow risk analysis. After table-level diffing, check hmtx (advance widths) and hhea/OS2 (line metrics) tables specifically. Compare per-glyph widths between reference and built fonts for shared glyphs. Categorize: none (all identical), line-spacing-only, minimal (tiny diffs), high (shared glyphs with different widths).
steps.details: This requires reading binary font tables. Options: (a) use read-fonts crate (already in the fontc workspace) to parse hmtx/hhea/OS2 tables directly in Rust — this is the cleanest approach and avoids Python, (b) shell out to Python fonttools. Option (a) is strongly preferred since read-fonts is already a dependency of the fontc workspace.

steps.description: Adapt the CI pipeline (ci.rs). The current flow: parse input JSON → check if rerun needed → precompile binaries → make targets → run diffs → save results → generate HTML. New flow: scan METADATA.pb files → check if rerun needed → ensure google/fonts clone is up-to-date → ensure upstream repos are cached → run builds+comparisons in parallel → save results → generate HTML + JSON for dashboard.
steps.details: Keep fontc_crater's RunSummary model (began/finished timestamps, revision tracking, result file references). Add new fields: google_fonts_rev (commit of google/fonts used), total_families, families_with_source, families_without_source. The parallel execution via Rayon can be kept as-is — it already handles per-repo locking and error isolation.

steps.description: Adapt result storage. Current format: Results<DiffOutput, DiffError> with per-target success/failure maps. New format: Results<ReproResult, BuildError> where ReproResult carries the categorization (byte-identical, compiler-version, etc.), per-file comparison details, and reflow risk. Output both JSON (for dashboard consumption) and HTML (for standalone viewing).
steps.details: The JSON output should be directly consumable by the gfonts_agents dashboard's sync_dashboard.py, replacing or supplementing build_registry.json. Schema: { families: { family_slug: { reproducible_build: category, files: { filename: { byte_identical, differing_tables, mismatch_category, reflow_risk } } } } }.

steps.description: Adapt HTML report generation (ci/html.rs). Replace the fontc-vs-fontmake comparison view with a reproducibility dashboard view: summary cards (byte-identical count, compiler-version count, failures, reflow risk), filterable table of all families, per-family detail pages showing table diffs and comparison details.
steps.details: fontc_crater uses the maud crate for HTML templating. The existing report structure (summary stats → table of results → detail modals) maps naturally to the reproducibility use case. Add color-coding: green for byte-identical, yellow for compiler-version, orange for reflow risk, red for build failure.

steps.description: Add a CLI mode for single-family testing. Current fontc_crater only runs in batch CI mode. Add a subcommand like 'check <family_slug>' that builds and compares a single family, printing results to stdout. This is essential for development iteration and debugging.

Integration testing. Run the prototype on a representative sample: (a) 10 byte-identical families from the Python system, (b) 10 compiler-version families, (c) 5 known build failures, (d) 5 families with reflow risk. Verify that the Rust tool produces the same categorizations as the Python system.
steps.details: Test families should include: roboto (large, complex), noto-sans (CJK, huge), lato (simple static), playfair-display (known build failure), artifika (known reflow risk). Compare JSON output field-by-field against existing build_registry.json entries.

key_modules.name: main.rs
key_modules.role: Entry point, CLI parsing (ci + check subcommands)

key_modules.name: target.rs
key_modules.role: FamilyTarget struct (family_slug, repo_url, commit, config, reference_binaries)

key_modules.name: metadata.rs
key_modules.role: METADATA.pb text-format parser (extract source block fields)

key_modules.name: discovery.rs
key_modules.role: Walk google/fonts dirs, parse METADATA.pb, build Vec<FamilyTarget>

key_modules.name: builder.rs
key_modules.role: Run gftools-builder (or fontc) on checked-out source, collect built fonts

key_modules.name: compare.rs
key_modules.role: SHA256 hash comparison, TTX table diffing, result categorization

key_modules.name: reflow.rs
key_modules.role: Reflow risk analysis using read-fonts (hmtx, hhea, OS/2 parsing)

key_modules.name: ci.rs
key_modules.role: Batch pipeline: discover → build → compare → report (adapted from fontc_crater)

key_modules.name: report.rs
key_modules.role: HTML report generation via maud (adapted from fontc_crater ci/html.rs)

key_modules.name: cache.rs
key_modules.role: Git repo cache management, build artifact caching (from fontc_crater)

reused_from_fontc_crater: Rayon-based parallel execution (run_all pattern from main.rs)
Git cache management (clone/fetch/checkout at specific commit)
Result caching (ci/results_cache.rs — cache fontmake artifacts by commit+config hash)
HTML report generation scaffolding (maud templates, summary stats, collapsible details)
RunSummary tracking (timestamps, input hashes, change detection for skip-if-unchanged)
new_components: Reference binary locator (map dest_file from METADATA.pb to google/fonts path)
ReproResult categorization engine (match differing tables to root cause categories)
Reflow risk analyzer (read-fonts to parse hmtx/hhea/OS2, compare advance widths)
Dashboard JSON output (schema compatible with sync_dashboard.py)

risks_and_open_questions.mitigation: Text-format protobuf for the fields we need (repository_url, commit, config_yaml, files) is simple enough for a line-based parser. No need for full protobuf support.

The prototype still shells out to gftools-builder for compilation. This means Python + gftools must be installed. The Rust tool's value comes from orchestration, not from replacing the compiler. A future phase could use fontc directly for compilation.

The categorization logic (byte-identical, compiler-version, etc.) must match the Python system's behavior exactly. Integration tests (step 10) are critical. Run both systems on the same families and diff the results.

risks_and_open_questions.risk: virtiofs FD accumulation affects both systems equally
risks_and_open_questions.mitigation: The virtiofs bottleneck is intrinsic to the VM setup, not the language. The Rust tool will hit the same FD limits as the Python system when running on this machine. Mitigations remain the same: periodic 'echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches', limiting concurrency to 2-3 parallel builds, and extracting to local tmpfs when possible.

risks_and_open_questions.risk: Maintenance burden of two parallel systems
risks_and_open_questions.mitigation: This is explicitly a prototype. The Python system remains the source of truth. The Rust tool is experimental. If it proves superior, it replaces the Python system; if not, it's abandoned with minimal cost.
fontc_crater already depends on google-fonts-sources 0.11 for repo discovery. The new tool can reuse this for git cache management but does its own target discovery via METADATA.pb.

rows: Sequential (1 family at a time, max 2-3 due to virtiofs)
Rayon threadpool (configurable, but same virtiofs limit applies on this VM — 2-3 concurrent in practice)
Git cache + build artifact cache (fontc_crater pattern)
Same virtiofs bottleneck applies; value is in unified tooling, not throughput

design_notes.title: Can the reproducible build system coexist with fontc_crater's compiler-testing scope?
design_notes.body:
After implementing the prototype as a separate crate (gfonts_repro_crater) within the fontc monorepo, the question arises: should this share a codebase with fontc_crater, or remain a separate fork? This analysis examines the actual code overlap, the architectural forces pulling toward and away from unification, and recommends a path forward.

What the two tools actually share

Looking at the implemented code, the shared infrastructure is real but thin:

  • run_all() parallel executor — Both use the same Rayon pattern: Vec of targets → parallel map → collect results. This is ~30 lines of generic code. Straightforward to extract into a shared crate, but also trivial to copy.
  • Results<T, E> struct — Both use BTreeMap-based success/failure maps with FromIterator. ~40 lines.
  • RunSummary tracking — Both save began/finished timestamps, a revision identifier, and a results_file path. The fields diverge (fontc_rev + pip_freeze_sha vs google_fonts_rev), but the pattern is the same.
  • HTML report generation — Both use maud + tidier. But fontc_crater's html.rs is 1,077 lines of highly specific compiler-comparison UI (delta bars, annotation modals, per-table diff rendering), while gfonts_repro_crater needs a reproducibility-focused UI (category cards, reflow risk, family-level status tables). Almost nothing transfers.
  • Utility functions — try_read_json, try_write_json, try_create_dir, human_readable_duration. ~60 lines of boilerplate.
  • Git cache management — fontc_crater delegates this entirely to google-fonts-sources crate (clone/fetch/checkout). gfonts_repro_crater uses its own simpler git commands since it reads from a pre-existing cache. Not actually shared code.

Total genuinely shared code: roughly 130 lines of utilities and patterns. Everything else diverges.

Where the two tools fundamentally diverge

Aspectfontc_cratergfonts_repro_crater
PurposeMeasure fontc parity with fontmakeVerify google/fonts can rebuild from source
Target unitSource file (one .glyphs/.ufo per target, multiple per repo)Font family (one family = one METADATA.pb, multiple font files)
Target discoveryHand-curated JSON list + google-fonts-sources crateWalk google/fonts METADATA.pb files
What gets comparedfontc output vs fontmake output (two builds of the same source)Rebuilt font vs shipped binary in google/fonts (one build vs one reference)
Comparison methodTTX XML diff via Python ttx_diff module (per-table similarity ratios)SHA256 hash + skrifa binary table comparison (per-table identical/different)
Result categoriesidentical / diffs (with ratio) / fontc_failed / fontmake_failed / both_failedbyte-identical / compiler-version / name-table / timestamp-diff / build-failure / reflow-risk
Python dependencyHeavy: ttx_diff, fontmake, fonttools, gftools all required at runtimeOnly gftools-builder for compilation; comparison is pure Rust
CI modelSkip-if-unchanged (fontc rev + pip freeze + input file SHA)Skip-if-unchanged (google/fonts rev)
Upstream maintainergooglefonts/fontc team (Colin Rofls et al.)Felipe / local experimental

Arguments FOR a shared codebase

  1. DRY principle: The ~130 lines of shared utilities would not be duplicated. In practice this is a weak argument — the duplication is trivial and the utilities are stable.
  2. Single binary: A unified tool could do both jobs with subcommands (crater ci, crater repro). Users wouldn't need to install two binaries. However, the user base is nearly disjoint: the fontc team runs fontc_crater in CI; we run gfonts_repro_crater locally.
  3. Shared improvements: If someone improves the parallel executor or caching in one tool, the other benefits automatically. True but speculative — these components are stable and unlikely to change often.
  4. Common Target type: In theory, both tools operate on "font source + config → built font". A sufficiently abstract Target type could serve both. But abstraction here adds complexity without reducing the amount of code needed.

Arguments AGAINST a shared codebase

  1. Different owners: fontc_crater is maintained by the fontc team at Google. gfonts_repro_crater is an experimental tool by Felipe. Merging them means every change to one tool requires consideration of the other, which slows both teams down. This is the strongest argument against sharing.
  2. Divergent evolution: fontc_crater is evolving toward fontc feature completeness. gfonts_repro_crater may evolve toward cohort version matching, custom venv management, monorepo support, and other features specific to the reproducibility problem. Sharing a codebase creates coupling between unrelated feature trajectories.
  3. Abstraction tax: Making Target, Results, comparison logic, and reporting generic enough to serve both use cases would require trait-based abstraction. The resulting code would be harder to read and modify than two simple, concrete implementations. For ~130 lines of shared code, the abstraction cost exceeds the duplication cost.
  4. Different Python dependencies: fontc_crater requires ttx_diff (and its full Python dependency tree including fontmake). gfonts_repro_crater only needs gftools-builder for the build step, and does comparison in pure Rust. Sharing a codebase would make it unclear which Python deps are needed for which mode.
  5. PR friction: fontc_crater lives in the googlefonts/fontc repo. Adding a Google Fonts reproducibility tool to that repo changes its scope. PRs would need review from the fontc team for a tool they don't use. This is a social/organizational cost, not a technical one, but it's real.
  6. HTML reports are completely different: The 1,077-line html.rs in fontc_crater renders compiler-comparison dashboards with delta bars, annotation modals, and per-table diff ratios. gfonts_repro_crater renders reproducibility category cards, reflow risk analysis, and family-level status tables. Zero reuse.

A middle path: shared library crate

One option is to extract the shared utilities (run_all, Results, try_read_json, etc.) into a small library crate (e.g., crater_common) that both tools depend on. This avoids duplicating the ~130 lines while keeping the tools otherwise independent.

Verdict: not worth it. 130 lines of stable, trivial utility code doesn't justify a shared crate with its own versioning, API stability guarantees, and coordination overhead. The standard Rust ecosystem approach for this amount of code is to just copy it.

Recommendation

Keep them separate. The prototype is correctly implemented as a standalone crate. The shared code is minimal and the divergence is fundamental — different targets, different comparisons, different reports, different maintainers, different evolution paths.

The current setup (gfonts_repro_crater as a separate crate in the fontc workspace) is a reasonable starting point for prototyping, but if this tool matures, it should move to its own repository (or into gfonts_agents) rather than remaining in the fontc monorepo. The fontc team shouldn't need to carry a tool they don't use.

Concrete next steps if proceeding:

  1. Keep prototyping in the fontc workspace for convenience (access to skrifa, read-fonts)
  2. When the tool is proven, move it to a standalone repo with skrifa/read-fonts as normal crate dependencies
  3. Do not attempt to merge with fontc_crater — the abstraction cost exceeds the duplication cost
